questions.question: A satellite stays in circular orbit around Earth because:
options: Its engines constantly push it forward
Earth's gravity provides the centripetal force
There is no gravity in space
questions.explanation: Earth's gravitational pull on the satellite acts as the centripetal force, keeping it in circular orbit.

questions.question: The orbital speed of a satellite depends on:
options: The radius of the orbit and the mass of the central body
The radius of the orbit only
The mass of the satellite and the radius
questions.explanation: $v = \sqrt{GM/r}$. Orbital speed depends on the mass of the central body ($M$) and the orbital radius ($r$), but not the satellite's mass.

questions.question: Satellite A orbits at radius $r$. Satellite B orbits at radius $4r$ around the same planet. How does B's speed compare to A's?
options: B has one-quarter the speed of A
B has half the speed of A
B has the same speed as A
B has twice the speed of A

questions.question: A satellite in a higher orbit has a:
options: Same period as a lower satellite
questions.explanation: $T \propto r^{3/2}$. A larger orbital radius means a longer orbital period.

questions.question: A geostationary satellite appears to hover over the same point on Earth's equator because:
options: Its orbital period equals Earth's rotation period (24 hours)
It moves in the opposite direction to Earth's rotation
questions.explanation: A geostationary satellite has an orbital period of 24 hours, matching Earth's rotation. It orbits above the equator, appearing fixed in the sky.

questions.question: Astronauts in orbit experience apparent weightlessness because:
options: There is no gravity in space
They and the spacecraft are in free fall together
They are beyond Earth's gravitational field
questions.explanation: Both the astronaut and spacecraft accelerate toward Earth at the same rate (free fall). With no normal force pushing back, the astronaut feels weightless.

questions.question: A satellite orbits Earth where the acceleration due to gravity is $1.6\text{ m/s}^2$ and the orbital radius is $1.0 \times 10^7\text{ m}$. What is its orbital speed?
Satellite X orbits at radius $r$ with period $T$. Satellite Y orbits at radius $4r$ around the same planet. What is Y's period?
Two satellites of different masses orbit at the same radius around Earth. Their orbital speeds are:
questions.explanation: $v = \sqrt{GM/r}$. The satellite's mass cancels out — orbital speed depends only on the central body's mass and the radius.

questions.question: If a satellite's orbital radius is doubled, its orbital period:
questions.explanation: $T \propto r^{3/2}$. Doubling $r$ means $T$ increases by $2^{3/2} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83$ times.

questions.question: Low Earth orbit satellites travel at approximately:
questions.explanation: Low Earth orbit speed is roughly $7,800\text{ m/s}$ (about $28,000\text{ km/h}$), completing one orbit in approximately 90 minutes.

questions.question: A satellite in low Earth orbit (radius ≈ $6,600\text{ km}$) has an orbital period closest to:
questions.explanation: Low Earth orbit period is approximately 90 minutes. The ISS, for example, orbits Earth about 16 times per day.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered satellite motion and orbital mechanics!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the relationships $v = \sqrt{GM/r}$ and $T \propto r^{3/2}$.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on how orbital speed and period change with radius.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: gravity as centripetal force, weightlessness in orbit, and geostationary satellites.
